channel.title: linear algebra on Personal Math Blog
channel.description: Recent content in linear algebra on Personal Math Blog

item.title: Linear Algebra: Tensor Product
item.description: Assume that all vector spaces in this post are over $\mathbb{K}$. Definition 1. Let $\mathcal{A}$ be a set. The vector space over $\mathbb{K}$ generated by $\mathcal{A}$ is defined to be the set $F(\mathcal{A})$ of all maps $\varphi: \mathcal{A} \to \mathbb{K}$ such that $\varphi(\alpha) = 0$ for all but only a finite number of $\alpha$ in $\mathcal{A}$. We could see that $$F(\mathcal{A})\cong \bigoplus\limits_{\alpha\in \mathcal{A}} \mathbb{K}$$ One could treat $\mathcal{A}$ as a basis for $F(\mathcal{A})$ under the embedding $$ \begin{alignat*}{2} j: \mathcal{A} & \to F(\mathcal{A}) \\ \alpha & \mapsto \mathbf{e}_{\alpha} \end{alignat*} $$ where $\mathbf{e}_{\alpha}(\beta) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if }\beta = \alpha \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$ for each $\alpha\in \mathcal{A}$.

item.title: Linear Algebra: Matrices and Algebraic Duals
item.description: Assume that all vector spaces in this post are vector spaces over a field $\mathbb{K}$. Coordinate, matrices 🔗 Definition 1. Let $V$ be a vector space of dimension $n$, let $(\alpha_1, \alpha_2, …, \alpha_n)$ be an ordered basis for $V$. For each vector $\mathbf{v}\in V$, we can write $\mathbf{v}$ uniquely as $$\mathbf{v} = a_1\cdot \alpha_1 + a_2\cdot \alpha_2 + … + a_n\cdot \alpha_n$$ Then the tuple $(a_1, a_2, …, a_n) \in \mathbb{K}^n$ is called the coordinate of $\mathbf{v}$ with respect to the ordered basis $(\alpha_1, \alpha_2, …, \alpha_n)$.

item.title: Linear Algebra: Basic construction
item.description: Vector spaces, subspaces, linear independence, bases 🔗 Definition 1. Let $\mathbb{K}$ be a field. A vector space over $\mathbb{K}$ is a non-empty set $V$ together with two operations $+ : V\times V \to V$ and $\cdot : \mathbb{K}\times V\to V$, called the vectors addition and the scalar multiplication, respectively, which satisfies the following axioms $(V, +)$ is an Abelian group, we denote the its identity element by $\mathbf{0}$. $1\cdot \mathbf{v} = \mathbf{v}$ for every $\mathbf{v}\in V$.
